questions.stem: For $1 \le p < \infty$, the $L^p$ norm of a measurable function $f$ on a measure space $(X, \mu)$ is defined as which quantity?
questions.explanation: The $L^p$ norm is $\|f\|_p = \left( \int_X |f|^p\,d\mu \right)^{1/p}$, the $p$-th root of the integral of $|f|^p$. Omitting the root gives $\|f\|_p^p$ instead, and the supremum corresponds to the separate $L^\infty$ norm.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions II course (MATH 462)

questions.stem: Two exponents $p$ and $q$ in $(1, \infty)$ are called conjugate exponents when which relation holds?
questions.explanation: Conjugate exponents satisfy $\frac{1}{p} + \frac{1}{q} = 1$, equivalently $q = \frac{p}{p-1}$. This is exactly the pairing under which Holder's inequality holds. The case $p = q = 2$ is self-conjugate and gives Cauchy-Schwarz.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions II course (MATH 462)

questions.stem: If $p = 3$, what is its conjugate exponent $q$ with $\frac{1}{p} + \frac{1}{q} = 1$?
questions.explanation: From $\frac{1}{3} + \frac{1}{q} = 1$ we get $\frac{1}{q} = \frac{2}{3}$, so $q = \frac{3}{2}$. Conjugate exponents always satisfy $q = \frac{p}{p-1}$, here $\frac{3}{2}$.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions II course (MATH 462)

questions.stem: Holder's inequality, for conjugate exponents $p, q$, bounds $\int_X |fg|\,d\mu$ by which product?
questions.explanation: Holder's inequality states $\int_X |fg|\,d\mu \le \|f\|_p\,\|g\|_q$ whenever $\frac{1}{p} + \frac{1}{q} = 1$. It bounds the $L^1$ norm of a product by the product of the $L^p$ and $L^q$ norms, not by a sum or by powered norms.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions II course (MATH 462)

questions.stem: The Cauchy-Schwarz inequality is the special case of Holder's inequality for which exponents?
questions.explanation: Taking $p = q = 2$ (which satisfies $\frac{1}{2} + \frac{1}{2} = 1$) turns Holder's inequality into $\int |fg| \le \|f\|_2\,\|g\|_2$, the Cauchy-Schwarz inequality. The exponent $2$ is the unique self-conjugate value.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions II course (MATH 462)

questions.stem: Minkowski's inequality, for $1 \le p < \infty$, is which statement?
questions.explanation: Minkowski's inequality is the triangle inequality for the $L^p$ norm: $\|f + g\|_p \le \|f\|_p + \|g\|_p$. It is what makes $\|\cdot\|_p$ an actual norm, and its proof typically uses Holder's inequality.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions II course (MATH 462)

questions.stem: Which inequality is the principal tool used to prove Minkowski's inequality for $L^p$?
questions.explanation: The standard proof of Minkowski writes $\|f+g\|_p^p = \int |f+g|^{p-1}|f+g|$ and splits $|f+g| \le |f| + |g|$, then applies Holder's inequality to each piece with exponents $p$ and $q = \frac{p}{p-1}$. Holder is thus the engine behind the triangle inequality.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions II course (MATH 462)

questions.stem: The essential supremum norm $\|f\|_\infty$ is defined as which quantity?
options: $\inf\{ M \ge 0 : |f(x)| \le M \text{ for almost every } x \}$.
questions.explanation: The essential supremum ignores behaviour on null sets: $\|f\|_\infty = \inf\{M \ge 0 : |f| \le M \text{ a.e.}\}$. It can be strictly smaller than the ordinary supremum if $f$ spikes on a set of measure zero.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions II course (MATH 462)

questions.stem: On a finite measure space with $\mu(X) < \infty$, which inclusion holds for $1 \le p \le r \le \infty$?
options: There is no inclusion between $L^p$ and $L^r$.
questions.explanation: On a finite measure space the higher-exponent space is smaller: $L^r \subseteq L^p$ for $p \le r$, because Holder's inequality gives $\|f\|_p \le \mu(X)^{1/p - 1/r}\,\|f\|_r$. The finiteness of $\mu(X)$ is essential; the inclusion can fail or reverse on infinite measure spaces.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions II course (MATH 462)

questions.stem: Why does the inclusion $L^r \subseteq L^p$ (for $p \le r$) require the measure space to have finite measure?
options: Because the constant $\mu(X)^{1/p - 1/r}$ in the bound $\|f\|_p \le \mu(X)^{1/p - 1/r}\|f\|_r$ is finite only when $\mu(X) < \infty$.
Because Holder's inequality fails on infinite measure spaces.
Because the norm is not defined otherwise.
questions.explanation: Applying Holder to $|f|^p = |f|^p \cdot 1$ with exponents $r/p$ and its conjugate yields $\|f\|_p \le \mu(X)^{1/p - 1/r}\,\|f\|_r$. This constant blows up when $\mu(X) = \infty$, so the embedding holds only on finite measure spaces; on the line, neither $L^p$ contains the other.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions II course (MATH 462)

questions.stem: For functions on a finite measure space, the conjugate exponent of $p = 2$ is which value?
questions.explanation: Since $\frac{1}{2} + \frac{1}{2} = 1$, the exponent $p = 2$ is its own conjugate, $q = 2$. This self-conjugacy is what gives $L^2$ its special inner-product structure via Cauchy-Schwarz.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions II course (MATH 462)

questions.stem: The conjugate exponent of $p = 1$ is which value, so that Holder still reads $\int |fg| \le \|f\|_1 \|g\|_\infty$?
questions.explanation: The pair $p = 1$ and $q = \infty$ is the limiting conjugate pair, with $\frac{1}{1} + \frac{1}{\infty} = 1$ interpreted via $\frac{1}{\infty} = 0$. Holder then reads $\int |fg|\,d\mu \le \|f\|_1\,\|g\|_\infty$, bounding the integral by the $L^1$ norm times the essential supremum.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions II course (MATH 462)

questions.stem: When does equality hold in Holder's inequality $\int |fg| \le \|f\|_p \|g\|_q$ (with $1 < p < \infty$)?
options: When $|f|^p$ and $|g|^q$ are proportional almost everywhere.
questions.explanation: Equality in Holder occurs exactly when $|f|^p$ and $|g|^q$ are proportional a.e., that is $\alpha |f|^p = \beta |g|^q$ for constants not both zero. This is the case where the underlying Young's inequality $ab \le \frac{a^p}{p} + \frac{b^q}{q}$ is tight.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions II course (MATH 462)

questions.stem: Young's inequality, which underlies Holder, states that for $a, b \ge 0$ and conjugate exponents $p, q$:
questions.explanation: Young's inequality reads $ab \le \frac{a^p}{p} + \frac{b^q}{q}$ for $\frac{1}{p} + \frac{1}{q} = 1$, with equality iff $a^p = b^q$. Integrating the pointwise version applied to normalized $|f|$ and $|g|$ yields Holder's inequality.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions II course (MATH 462)

questions.stem: Let $f = \mathbf{1}_{[0,1]}$ on $[0,1]$ with Lebesgue measure (so $f \equiv 1$). What is $\|f\|_p$ for any $1 \le p < \infty$?
questions.explanation: Here $\|f\|_p = \left( \int_0^1 1^p\,dx \right)^{1/p} = 1^{1/p} = 1$ for every $p$. A normalized indicator on a unit-measure set has all $L^p$ norms equal to $1$.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions II course (MATH 462)

questions.stem: Compute $\|f\|_2$ for $f(x) = x$ on $[0,1]$ with Lebesgue measure.
questions.explanation: We have $\|f\|_2^2 = \int_0^1 x^2\,dx = \frac{1}{3}$, so $\|f\|_2 = \sqrt{1/3} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$. The value $\frac{1}{3}$ is $\|f\|_2^2$, before taking the square root.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions II course (MATH 462)

questions.stem: Which statement about the relationship between $\|\cdot\|_p$ and the triangle inequality is correct?
options: The triangle inequality for $\|\cdot\|_p$ is exactly Minkowski's inequality.
The triangle inequality for $\|\cdot\|_p$ is exactly Holder's inequality.
The triangle inequality only holds for $p = 1$.
questions.explanation: Minkowski's inequality $\|f + g\|_p \le \|f\|_p + \|g\|_p$ is precisely the triangle inequality for the $L^p$ norm, valid for all $1 \le p \le \infty$. Holder's inequality, by contrast, governs products, not sums.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions II course (MATH 462)

questions.stem: For $0 < p < 1$, why is $\|\cdot\|_p$ generally NOT a norm?
options: Because the triangle inequality (Minkowski) fails for $0 < p < 1$.
questions.explanation: For $0 < p < 1$ Minkowski's inequality reverses, so $\|\cdot\|_p$ fails the triangle inequality and is only a quasi-norm. The convexity arguments behind Minkowski require $p \ge 1$.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions II course (MATH 462)

questions.stem: In $\|f\|_p = 0$ for $f \in L^p$, what does this force about $f$?
options: $f$ is identically zero at every point.
questions.explanation: Since $\|f\|_p^p = \int |f|^p\,d\mu = 0$ with $|f|^p \ge 0$, the integrand must vanish a.e., so $f = 0$ almost everywhere. This is why $L^p$ elements are equivalence classes modulo null sets, not genuine pointwise functions.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions II course (MATH 462)

questions.stem: Apply Holder's inequality on $[0,1]$ with $p = q = 2$ to bound $\int_0^1 |fg|\,dx$ for $f(x) = x$ and $g(x) = 1$. Which bound results?
questions.explanation: Here $\|f\|_2 = \frac{1}{\sqrt 3}$ and $\|g\|_2 = 1$, so Cauchy-Schwarz gives $\int_0^1 |fg| \le \|f\|_2\|g\|_2 = \frac{1}{\sqrt 3}$. The actual integral $\int_0^1 x\,dx = \frac{1}{2}$ indeed satisfies $\frac{1}{2} \le \frac{1}{\sqrt 3} \approx 0.577$.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions II course (MATH 462)

questions.stem: Which statement correctly distinguishes Holder's and Minkowski's inequalities?
options: Holder bounds a product $\int |fg|$; Minkowski bounds a sum $\|f + g\|_p$.
Minkowski requires conjugate exponents; Holder does not.
questions.explanation: Holder controls the integral of a product, $\int |fg| \le \|f\|_p\|g\|_q$ with conjugate exponents; Minkowski controls the norm of a sum, $\|f+g\|_p \le \|f\|_p + \|g\|_p$ at a single exponent. It is Holder, not Minkowski, that needs conjugate exponents.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions II course (MATH 462)

questions.stem: For the counting measure on $\mathbb{N}$ (giving the sequence spaces $\ell^p$), which inclusion holds for $1 \le p \le r$?
questions.explanation: On counting measure the inclusion goes the other way from finite measure spaces: $\ell^p \subseteq \ell^r$ for $p \le r$, since $\sum |a_n|^p < \infty$ forces $|a_n| \to 0$ and hence $\sum |a_n|^r < \infty$. This reversal illustrates that the direction of $L^p$ inclusions depends critically on the measure.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions II course (MATH 462)

questions.stem: Which is a correct restatement of the conjugacy relation $\frac{1}{p} + \frac{1}{q} = 1$?
questions.explanation: Solving $\frac{1}{q} = 1 - \frac{1}{p} = \frac{p-1}{p}$ gives $q = \frac{p}{p-1}$. For example $p = 4$ yields $q = \frac{4}{3}$, consistent with $\frac{1}{4} + \frac{3}{4} = 1$.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions II course (MATH 462)

questions.stem: Holder's inequality can be generalized to three functions with exponents $p, q, r$ satisfying which condition?
questions.explanation: The generalized Holder inequality $\int |fgh| \le \|f\|_p\|g\|_q\|h\|_r$ holds when $\frac{1}{p} + \frac{1}{q} + \frac{1}{r} = 1$. It extends the two-function conjugacy $\frac{1}{p} + \frac{1}{q} = 1$ to any finite number of factors whose reciprocal exponents sum to one.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions II course (MATH 462)

questions.stem: Conjugate exponents and the named inequalities.
questions.solution: Two exponents are conjugate when the sum of their reciprocals equals {{1}}. The inequality $\int_X |fg|\,d\mu \le \|f\|_p\,\|g\|_q$ that uses conjugate exponents is called {{2}}. The inequality $\|f + g\|_p \le \|f\|_p + \|g\|_p$, which is the triangle inequality for the $L^p$ norm, is called {{3}}.
questions.explanation: Conjugate exponents satisfy $\frac{1}{p} + \frac{1}{q} = 1$, a reciprocal sum of $1$. The product bound $\int |fg| \le \|f\|_p\|g\|_q$ is Holder's inequality, and the sum bound $\|f+g\|_p \le \|f\|_p + \|g\|_p$ is Minkowski's inequality.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions II course (MATH 462)

questions.stem: Computing conjugate exponents. Use $\frac{1}{p} + \frac{1}{q} = 1$.
questions.solution: If $p = 4$, then its conjugate exponent $q$ equals the fraction $4/{{1}}$. If $p = 2$, the conjugate exponent is {{2}}, which is why $L^2$ is self-dual. As $p \to 1$, the conjugate exponent $q$ tends to {{3}}.
questions.explanation: For $p = 4$, $q = \frac{p}{p-1} = \frac{4}{3}$, so the missing denominator is $3$. For $p = 2$, $q = 2$ (self-conjugate). As $p \to 1^+$, $q = \frac{p}{p-1} \to \infty$, giving the limiting pair $(1, \infty)$.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions II course (MATH 462)

questions.stem: An $L^p$ norm computation. Let $f(x) = x$ on $[0,1]$ with Lebesgue measure.
questions.solution: The square $\|f\|_2^2 = \int_0^1 x^2\,dx$ evaluates to the fraction $1/{{1}}$. The first power norm $\|f\|_1 = \int_0^1 x\,dx$ equals the fraction $1/{{2}}$. Because the measure of $[0,1]$ is one, the inclusion direction among these spaces is that $L^2$ on $[0,1]$ is contained in {{3}}.
questions.explanation: Compute $\int_0^1 x^2\,dx = \frac{1}{3}$ and $\int_0^1 x\,dx = \frac{1}{2}$, so the denominators are $3$ and $2$. On the finite measure space $[0,1]$, higher exponents give smaller spaces, so $L^2 \subseteq L^1$.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions II course (MATH 462)

questions.stem: Special cases and structure of $L^p$.
questions.solution: The exponent for which the $L^p$ norm comes from an inner product is $p = $ {{1}}. The Holder inequality at $p = q = 2$ is known by the name {{2}}. The norm $\|f\|_\infty$ is the essential {{3}} of $|f|$, ignoring sets of measure zero.
questions.explanation: Only $L^2$ has a norm induced by an inner product $\langle f, g \rangle = \int f\bar g$, so $p = 2$. Holder at $p = q = 2$ is the Cauchy-Schwarz inequality, and $\|f\|_\infty$ is the essential supremum of $|f|$.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions II course (MATH 462)
